title: Gradually Adding Noise to an Image
concept: The forward process takes a clean image and adds a small amount of random noise at each of many steps, slowly degrading it.

exercises.prompt: What happens if you slowly add drops of black paint into a cup of white paint, one drop at a time?

options.text: It slowly turns grayer and grayer until it's all dark
It instantly turns black after one drop

exercises.explain: Right! Each drop only changes the paint a tiny bit, but after many drops, the color has completely changed. That's exactly the idea behind adding noise step by step.

cards.title: How Do You Break a Photo... Slowly?
cards.body: Imagine taking a clear photo of your dog and messing it up bit by bit, until it's just fuzzy random dots. Why would anyone want to do that on purpose? It turns out this 'slow breaking' trick is the secret first step behind how AI learns to create brand new images!

cards.title: The Forward Process
cards.body: The forward process is a series of small steps where a tiny bit of random noise (random speckles) is added to an image at each step. After enough steps, the clear image turns into pure random static, like TV snow.

cards.title: Like an Ice Cube Melting
cards.body: Think of an ice cube slowly melting into a puddle. It doesn't happen instantly — it melts a tiny bit at a time until, eventually, all that's left is a shapeless puddle. Adding noise to an image works the same way: small changes, repeated many times, until the original shape is gone.

cards.title: A Cat Photo Fading Away
cards.body: Step 1: A crisp photo of a cat. Step 50: The cat looks a little grainy, like an old TV. Step 200: You can barely make out the cat's shape through the fuzz. Step 1000: It's just random speckled noise — no cat visible at all. Each step only added a *little* noise, but together they erased the whole picture.

cards.title: Myth: Noise Is Added All at Once
cards.body: Nope! It's not like dumping a bucket of static on the image in one go. The forward process adds just a small sprinkle of noise at each of many steps — sometimes hundreds or thousands of tiny steps — so the change is gradual, not sudden.

cards.title: Clear Image vs. Noisy Image
cards.body: The forward process moves an image from one side to the other, one small noisy step at a time.

left.text: A sharp, clear picture with no noise at all

right.text: Random speckled static with no recognizable image left

exercises.prompt: What does the forward process do to an image?

options.text: Adds a small amount of random noise at each of many steps
Makes the image bigger in size

exercises.explain: The forward process is all about gradually adding tiny bits of noise, step after step, until the image becomes random static.

exercises.prompt: The forward process adds all the noise to an image in just one single step.
exercises.explain: False! Noise is added gradually, a tiny bit at a time, over many steps — not all at once.

exercises.sentence: In the forward process, a clean image is ___ turned into random noise through many small steps.
exercises.explain: The key word is 'gradually' — the change happens slowly, step by step, not all at once.

exercises.prompt: Which of these best describes what an image looks like halfway through the forward process (like step 500 out of 1000)?

options.text: Perfectly clear, exactly like the original photo
Somewhat grainy and fuzzy, with the original shape getting harder to see
Completely blank white with nothing on it

exercises.explain: Halfway through, the image has had noise added many times already, so it looks grainy and the original details start to blur, but it's not yet total random static.

exercises.prompt: Which everyday example best matches the idea of the forward process (adding noise gradually)?

options.text: A light switch flipping instantly from off to on
A photo of a mountain slowly getting covered by fog, a little more each minute, until you can't see the mountain at all
A balloon popping suddenly with one loud bang
A book being closed in one quick motion

exercises.explain: Just like fog slowly covering a mountain bit by bit until it's fully hidden, the forward process adds tiny amounts of noise over many steps until the original image is completely hidden in static.
